issue_title: Consider adding whisper.cpp and faster-whisper to comparison
issue_body:
Both run on CPU.

[whisper.cpp](https://github.com/ggerganov/whisper.cpp) is a port of Whisper in C++, it's now probably one of the most popular open-source speech-to-text models running offline on CPU. With the smallest `tiny` model (75 MB) it's 5-6 times slower than Leopard in my tests, though.

[faster-whisper](https://github.com/guillaumekln/faster-whisper) is a reimplementation of Whisper model using [CTranslate2](https://github.com/OpenNMT/CTranslate2/). It's not as popular yet, but it's 5 times faster than whisper.cpp on the same model, which makes it comparable to Leopard in terms of speed (even though it used 75 MB `tiny` model), in my tests.
issue_comments: @shvchk please feel free to submit a PR for any new engines you like. we are happy to look into them.

issue_title: Using multiple models from the same provider with different base urls and api keys
issue_body:
I am trying to evaluate a gpt-4o-mini model from azure openai using a gpt-4o scorer also from azure openai. These models have different base urls and api keys.

When trying this I faced the issue that the package expects both these models to share the same environment variables names, AZUREAI_OPENAI_API_KEY and AZUREAI_OPENAI_BASE_URL, even though the values are different for each model.

I tried to solve this by explicitly passing my base url and api key to my scorer model using `get_model`, but it doesn't work.

Like this:
```
        scorer=model_graded_fact(
            model=get_model(
                'openai/gpt-4o',
                config=GenerateConfig(temperature=0.0),
                base_url=judge_base_url,
                api_key=api_key,
            ),
            template=CUSTOM_GRADER_TEMPLATE,
            instructions=CUSTOM_GRADER_INSTRUCTIONS,
            grade_pattern=CUSTOM_GRADER_REGEX,
        )
```

Do you have any suggestions for how I can use the two different azure openai models in the same task?
issue_comments:
To use Azure models use the `open/azure` prefix (this was a recent-ish change: https://inspect.ai-safety-institute.org.uk/providers.html#openai-on-azure)

That should get you into the Azure codepath which will hopefully handle the multiple models correctly

issue_title: Support passing file-like objects (StringIO, BytesIO) to `read_eval_log` and similar functions
issue_body: Currently, `log_file` expects a `str` path.
issue_comments: The abstraction supported for `read_eval_log` is a "location" which today could be a local file but could also be a remote filesystem (s3, gs, etc.). Further, we've tried to be careful that "location" is never strictly treated as a file path so we can later support e.g. Prisma-style database connection URLs.  So taking file like doesn't fit with the abstractions and interfaces we've developed around `location` (which don't even take `Path` but rather just `str`). We could of course just take the file like object and dump it into a temp file, but the client could also do that and I think it's better if the client knows that this is happening vs. us doing it behind the scenes while _appearing_ to take the stream directly w/o copying or extra serialization.

issue_comments: It would also be good to be able to sort / filter by one (or more) of the scores

issue_title: Eval-retry only showing tokens used for current samples.
issue_body:
Currently if you use the command `eval-retry` to continue and evaluation it only counts the tokens used for the current samples. For example if you used 3M tokens to complete 3/4 samples in an eval and then stopped the eval and restarted it using `eval-retry` and completed the remaining 1 sample using 1M tokens the second log file produced by the retry would only show 1M tokens as being used.

If you don't know this you could use the second log file to estimate the number of tokens used in future runs and underestimate the tokens required. I think it should either not reset the tokens used between runs or show both the tokens used for the current run and the running total or just make it clearer in the log file that this is only the tokens used for the current sample and which samples those are.

interested to see what people think the best fix for this is.
issue_comments: I think maybe eval-retry should save the token usage before calling eval, then just amend the returned token usage which comes back from eval.
okay cool ill have a look at creating a PR for this.

issue_title: Support for evals within a module
issue_body:
I like to write my applications as modules, and I like to place my tests inside my modules so that I can easily import code (and not have to have the module on the path). Lots of test frameworks support this, however if I run run inspect on the files, I'll get errors about the module imports not being find (whether using absolute or relative imports)

For example

```
app/
    prompts/foo.py
    evals/eval_foo.py
    __init__.py
```

`eval_foo.py`

```python
from app.prompts.foo import prompt # ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'app'
from ..prompts.foo import prompt # ImportError: attempted relative import beyond top-level package
```

I can somewhat workaround this by adding to the python path:

```bash
PYTHONPATH=./ inspect eval app/evals/eval_foo.py
```

In this case absolute imports now work.

Another workaround for absolute imports is to add to the python path in the eval file

```python
import os
import sys

sys.path.append(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))))

from app.prompts.foo import prompt
```

However it would be nice if inspect could support modules natively. I've come to expect this behavior from other python testing frameworks
issue_comments:
Thanks for this! We are _attempting_ to do something along the lines of what you are suggesting but apparently all the dots aren't connected. We have a `chdir_python()` context manager that we use when loading and running tasks, e.g.:

https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai/blob/main/src/inspect_ai/_eval/task/run.py#L98

This is turn adds to the `sys.path`:

https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai/blob/main/src/inspect_ai/_util/path.py#L40

Does anything about our scheme immediately strike you as deficient or otherwise not aligning with your scenario?

I think that should work?--I'm not an expert on this. I'd be curious to verify whether relative imports work.

As far as the logic to change directory, it seems like its happening too late, after the "task" object has been created from loading up the source code. Full stack trace from this reproduction https://github.com/adrianlyjak/inspect-ai-48-repro below. The failure happens within `resolve_tasks` here when calling `exec_module`

  File "/home/adrianlyjak/dev/inspect-ai-48-repro/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/inspect_ai/_eval/loader.py", line 115, in create_tasks
    tasks.extend(create_file_tasks(file, model, None, task_args))
                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/adrianlyjak/dev/inspect-ai-48-repro/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/inspect_ai/_eval/loader.py", line 134, in create_file_tasks
    task_specs = _load_task_specs(file)
                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/adrianlyjak/dev/inspect-ai-48-repro/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/inspect_ai/_eval/loader.py", line 158, in _load_task_specs
    module = load_task_module(task_path)
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/adrianlyjak/dev/inspect-ai-48-repro/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/inspect_ai/_eval/loader.py", line 188, in load_task_module
    loader.exec_module(module)
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap_external>", line 940, in exec_module
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap>", line 241, in _call_with_frames_removed
  File "/home/adrianlyjak/dev/inspect-ai-48-repro/app/evals/eval_foo.py", line 4, in <module>
    from app.prompts.foo import GREET
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'app'
```

Thanks for the repro!

We actually also change the directory during task loading (if you chase up the call stack you will see this).

I think the problem comes from the fact that you app is in `app/evals` and is attempting to import from the top of `app` (but we've added `evals` to the `sys.path` rather than `app/evals`. I'm not sure if there is a general way to determine what the "right" directory to add here is.

So it looks like we are very much assuming that the evals are at the "top" of the module structure (i.e. helpers would be in sub-directories off of where the eval is). I'm again not sure what a general resolution of this would look like (very open to suggestions!)

ah, I see. I have no immediate ideas (I haven't looked into importlib much before), but I can poke around pytest source code and see if there's something to learn from that.

If there's something useful there, its probably around here:

https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/5037f8d1145b87b0008902487ace44653692374b/src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py#L98

actually, maybe more like around here:

https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/5037f8d1145b87b0008902487ace44653692374b/src/_pytest/pathlib.py#L619

and pytest documentation on behavior https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/5037f8d1145b87b0008902487ace44653692374b/doc/en/explanation/pythonpath.rst#id10 😵‍💫

We aren't directly implementing import (and we don't have a notion of what the "root" directory scope is) so it might be hard to emulate pytest exactly. One thing we could do is search up the directory hierarchy looking for an `__init__.py` and add that to the `sys.path`. Do you think that would work?

@aisi-inspect I think that seems like the most straightforward method to get the full path (non relative) module imports working with in-module . Were you thinking something like, find the outermost folder that contains an `__init__.py`, and add it's parent directory to the `sys.path`? There's an unfortunate caveat where if you have your folder structured with your evals are in a sibling directory to your sources that the module imports wouldn't work, which also seems like its another test folder structure in python.

This kind of structure:

```
root/
    evals/
        eval_baz.py # has something like `from app.foo.baz import something`
    app/
        __init__.py
        foo/baz.py
```

In that case, it seems like there'd need to be some sort of a concept of a "root" directory to handle more flexible test structures. I don't know if that kind of flexibility is a goal of this project. I think it seems perfectly reasonable to be opinionated and just document that evals must be within the module to be automatically handled.

Hello hello, I've done a quick investigation of this. Please correct me if I'm wrong, but I'm under the impression that this is not an issue with inspect but a symptom of the fact that you haven't set up `app` as an installable package. 

The first thing I tried was to remove all of the inspect-related code. You can see that on my branch [rs-01-remove-inspect](https://github.com/adrianlyjak/inspect-ai-48-repro/compare/main...rusheb-apollo:inspect-ai-48-repro:rs-01-remove-inspect?expand=1). I keep your folder structure but simply define a python function `greet()` which attempts to import the GREET variable from `app`. It fails with the same error you provide in the README.

Second, in branch [rs-02-install-package](https://github.com/adrianlyjak/inspect-ai-48-repro/compare/main...rusheb-apollo:inspect-ai-48-repro:rs-02-install-package?expand=1) I add to your original repo a `pyproject.toml` file which sets up `app` as an installable package. The only change to the setup is to run `pip install -e .` rather than `pip install -r requirements.txt`. With those changes it seems like inspect can import from `app`.

Finally in branch [rs-03-sibling](https://github.com/adrianlyjak/inspect-ai-48-repro/compare/main...rusheb-apollo:inspect-ai-48-repro:rs-03-sibling?expand=1) I tested this with the sibling package structure which you mention in your previous comment. I tried this with a fresh `venv` and it seems to work just fine as well.

@adrianlyjak do you think this resolves your issue, or is there something I've got wrong here or a reason you wouldn't want to set up `app` as a package?
Thanks @rusheb , I seem to have overlooked that! That's definitely a reasonable solution.

issue_title: [feature request] make `subtask` nodes collapsible in the viewer
issue_body: Currently a task output with subtasks looks something like this: For tasks with many subtasks and/or where the summary/transcript is very long, it would be really helpful to be able to collapse the `SUBTASK` node via a toggle in the top left corner like we can do for the parent nodes. Currently I work around this by adding `transcript.step()` to the subtask, but this creates a lot of nesting and clicking

issue_title: Modern dependency management
issue_body:
In my view, the way this repo handles its dependencies is a weak point. Dependencies are largely unversioned.

This makes bugs like these an inevitability:
- https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai/issues/695
- https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai/issues/41

If you're open to it, I think it would be great to switch to `poetry` for dependency resolution (or other modern tooling if you have a preference). I'm happy to handle making the change (including necessary changes to CI workflows etc). But for such a change to the core tooling I'd want the maintainers' blessing before I make PR.
issue_comments:
If you're unfamiliar with `poetry`, here are two key pages to get you started:

* https://python-poetry.org/docs/basic-usage/#installing-dependencies
* https://python-poetry.org/docs/dependency-specification/
There are several advantages to using `poetry`, but if you don't want to use new tools, I think the absolute minimum that should be done is to specify dependency versions more tightly using version specifiers like `>=`, `<`, `~=`. See https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers
Our approach to dependency versions is to be liberal on purpose _until_ proven that we need a narrower dependency. The reason for this is that if too many libraries bound their versions then package solvers (conda, pip, uv, etc.) often can't resolve  situations where multiple packages are aggressively bounding. Alternatively, when upper bounds are used often solvers fall back to very old versions. We want to give solvers latitude to fit us into a deployment in an optimal fashion, again only applying version bounds when we have evidence that we need one.

The problem of excessive upper-bounding pinning is not unique to Poetry but it does seem to have been encouraged/facilitated by it. Here is a well reasoned critique of this practice that explains why it scales so poorly to a large ecosystem of libraries: https://iscinumpy.dev/post/bound-version-constraints/

Because of the above, packages necessarily need to take a different approach than deployments to versions. Deployments (i.e. your specific application and mix of packages) should hard lock onto package versions so you are reproducible and aren't exposed to random breakage. Packages though to be good citizens need to be as flexible as possible in their version requirements (even to the point of dynamically adapting to different versions of packages they depend on).

In terms of examples, this issue (https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai/issues/41) was evidence for needing a narrower requirement so we applied it. For the Google issue it's an optional dependency but we do require at runtime version 0.8.1 or higher (the fact that they made a breaking change in 0.8.3 is hard to solve for in advance!). We could hard bind to 0.8.1 but that's arbitrary and users could be missing important bug fixes if we do that. I'm more inclined to rely on package authors to have some modicum of respect for semantic versioning (and then tack when they don't).

Currently, our core requirements.txt does not do very much version bounding (except for where we've seen evidence its required like `typing_extensions`, `s3fs`, etc.) I am open to adding some lower bounds here if there are known issues they will help us avoid. In terms of optional dependencies, there we are more aggressive about lower bounding (typically requiring package versions from the past few months). You can see those requirements applied here in the `MIN_VERSION` constants for each provider: https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai/blob/main/src/inspect_ai/model/_providers/providers.py.





Thanks for the thoughtful reply!

Definitely agreed that deployments should install dependencies from a lockfile, while libraries should specify much looser (or possibly no) constraints. 

I especially liked the ISciNumpy.dev post. I wasn't familiar with the debate about whether upper bounds should ever be used, and it's shifted my perspective. Thanks!

I think there are two distinct questions for inspect:
1. Should the inspect repo use a lockfile (e.g. `poetry.lock`)?
2. How should dependencies be specified in `pyproject.toml`?

(Technically, a third question is: which dependency resolution algorithm is best? Poetry and pip each have their own dependency resolution algorithm. Historically, `pip` had a much less sophisticated dependency resolver. However, my vague impression is that with `pip>=22.0`, which uses `resolvelib`, it has caught up significantly. I was not able to quickly find more useful information about the pros and cons of modern `pip` and `poetry` resolution algorithms.)

**Should the inspect repo use a lockfile?**
My answer would be a strong yes. I think _any_ project that is (or ever might be) installed more than once should have a lockfile. This includes all but the most trivial projects. For example:

* if your project has more than one engineer working on it
* if you'll ever install the project on a new laptop
* if you have a CI pipeline

Without a lockfile, you do not have reproducible environments to develop the package or run tests. You are fully at the mercy of external changes in the dependency resolution. It's the dreaded 'it works on my machine'.

To express the same thing in terms of your deployment/library distinction: yes, inspect is a library, but every engineer's laptop, and GitHub actions, are 'deployments' that will be subject to random breakage without a lockfile.

Even for users of inspect who are not directly developing inspect, a lockfile is very useful when investigating a bug that might be caused by a dependency mismatch. The lockfile gives a 'known good' version of dependencies that inspect has been tested with. If we take the example of https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai/issues/695, I suspected a dependency problem as the cause, but I had no way of checking what version of `google-generativeai` inspect had been tested with in your CI pipeline. Proving that the bug was (or that it was not) due to dependency versioning would have involved relatively painstaking trial and error, so I didn't do it. With a lockfile, this becomes trivial. In the case of https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai/issues/695, I could have told you immediately in the issue report that it was a dependency specification problem.

**How should dependencies be specified?**

> We could hard bind to 0.8.1 but that's arbitrary and users could be missing important bug fixes if we do that.

I completely agree, we don't want to pin `==0.8.1`. I don't think anyone is advocating for pinning exact versions in `pyproject.toml`. I think that would be terrible! 

As far as I can tell, the positions in this debate are:

- The ISciNumpy.dev post argues that upper bounds should only be used when a newer version of a dependency is proven to be incompatible. Otherwise, they should never be used, not even for major versions.
- Poetry's default is [caret (`^`) versioning](https://python-poetry.org/docs/dependency-specification/#caret-requirements), which allows updates that are (supposed to be) compatible under semantic versioning.

I'm a bit confused about what your position is on this. Do you want to use semantic versioning or do you want to use no upper bounds? 

On the one hand, you linked to the ISciNumpy.dev post, and said in https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai/issues/695 "we are going to avoid upper-bounding like the plauge". On the other hand, you said in this thread "I'm more inclined to rely on package authors to have some modicum of respect for semantic versioning", which makes it sound like you do want to use semantic versioning. These seem like contradictory positions. 

As I said, I got a lot out of the ISciNumpy.dev post, and it did shift my views. I'm not sure I'm persuaded of the strongest version of its claims (no upper-bounding at all). I think there is still a trade-off here, between breakage caused by the lack of upper bound, and breakage caused by insufficient flexibility.

The strongest argument made by the ISciNumpy.dev post is that overly specific constraints cannot be fixed downstream, while insufficiently specific constraints can be fixed downstream once identified:

> If a library requires pyparsing>=3 and another library requests pyparsing<3, that’s the end, you are out of business.

This means there is a strong asymmetry between the two types of breakage: breakage caused by being too specific, when it occurs, is much worse. However, this could still be worth it, if the breakage caused by being too specific is much rarer. I could imagine this being the case if you only restrict major versions.

In any event, if you have a strong view on this topic I'm happy to defer to it.

Also FWIW, I started having a brief look at what very popular Python packages (by stars on GitHub) do:

- `scikit-learn`: [no upper-bounds](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/934df30a7f976e2fd902b39fb0ff1c026b9c230c/pyproject.toml#L9C1-L14C2) 
- `django`: [no upper-bounds](https://github.com/django/django/blob/99dcc59237f384d7ade98acfd1cae8d90e6d60ab/pyproject.toml#L9-L13) 
- `fastapi`: [upper-bounds starlette and pydantic](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/febf6b6a52e36e0d64920fb54670ad7fc29b2eaa/pyproject.toml#L44-L46). At a glance, neither seem to me to be due to known incompatibility.
  - `starlette`: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12431
  - `pedantic`: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/9816/files#diff-50c86b7ed8ac2cf95bd48334961bf0530cdc77b5a56f852c5c61b89d735fd711

- `flask`: [no upper-bounds](https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/2fec0b206c6e83ea813ab26597e15c96fab08be7/pyproject.toml#L23C1-L30C2) 
- `requests`: [upper-bounds 3 out of 4 of its dependencies](https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/7335bbf480adc8e6aa88feb2022797a549a00aa3/setup.cfg#L7-L10). At a glance, none seem to be due to known incompatibility. 
  - `charset_normalizer`: https://github.com/psf/requests/pull/6261
  - `idna`: https://github.com/psf/requests/pull/4889
  - `urllib3`: https://github.com/psf/requests/pull/6545
- `mitmproxy`: uses [very tight bounding for almost all its dependencies](https://github.com/mitmproxy/mitmproxy/blob/8ef97b1f6786cde5b79789d0ef903c47d4951eb3/pyproject.toml#L34-L62). These are not due to known incompatibilities but rather seem to be some `dependabot` policy. If you do a git blame on the lines that specify the dependencies you can see that the changes were authored by `dependabot`
- `scrapy`: [no upper-bounds](https://github.com/scrapy/scrapy/blob/6d65708cb7f7b49b72fc17486fecfc1caf62e0af/setup.py#L8-L25)

> Without a lockfile, you do not have reproducible environments to develop the package or run tests. You are fully at the mercy of external changes in the dependency resolution. It's the dreaded 'it works on my machine'.
> 
> To express the same thing in terms of your deployment/library distinction: yes, inspect is a library, but every engineer's laptop, and GitHub actions, are 'deployments' that will be subject to random breakage without a lockfile.

Have worked on many projects with and without lockfiles (the vast majority of our projects at AISI do in fact use Poetry lockfiles). In the case of Inspect, we are in some ways courting the variability so that we can discover issues with both newer and older versions as they come up. i.e. our users will not have lockfiles they'll just have a combination of older versions + very up to date versions. We want to expose ourselves to this same variability so we can proactively discover issues. 

That said, if we regularly experienced pain associated with not being able to get/keep things running we'd absolutely go for lockfiles. So far it has been more of a benefit than a hinderance but we will happily shift gears if it becomes obvious we should.

> I'm a bit confused about what your position is on this. Do you want to use semantic versioning or do you want to use no upper bounds?

When I say "semantic versioning" that's actually shorthand for just relying on the general practice of libraries doing their best not to cause breakage (I don't literally mean we will assume that semantic versioning works, which the referenced post does a good job pointing out doesn't really in practice). We allow a wide range of versions, then when we see issues attempt to resolve them by being maximally permissive (i.e. even dynamically supporting multiple versions if possible). Breakage happens, but so far we've only seen it from model API packages (which are often evolved alongside service changes) which is why we do more aggressively lower bounding there. 

Philosophically, we really try to make our software "fit" the user's environment even if it causes us some inconvenience. I've seen too many projects struggle with adoption due to overly specific requirements. Developers of packages sometimes think their package is the center of the universe when that couldn't be further from the truth from a user standpoint!

All of that said, I think we currently suffer from not quite enough lower bounding, as often picking reasonable (e.g. ~ 2 year old) lower bounds can help users avoid the bugs and compatibility issues that tend to be addressed over time. We just need to set aside the time to make some good judgements there.



I went through each dependency to check to see if a good case could be made for a lower-bound. In 2 or 3 cases we actually had a hard requirement from a version (typically though 2 or 3 yrs old though so it didn't seem to come up in practice). In several other cases packages have had releases that take care of Python or OS compatibility issues or made material improvements to stability, and I set lower bounds in those cases as well.

Updated requirements.txt in this commit: https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai/pull/736

issue_title: [QUESTION] Does COMET support multiple references?
issue_body: Thanks for the tool! I'm wondering if COMET supports multiple references, or if we can just score each sentence with all the references and take the maximum value? Sorry if this has already been mentioned somewhere.
issue_comments: Hi! This is actually an open question. We did some experiments where we tried to experiment with multiple references and from our experiments, a simple average is what works best. We found that using a reliable reference is actually more important than having multiple references (if they are of lower quality)

issue_title: Do you have any plans to test Sora2 on Vbench?
Empirical Min
issue_body:
Thank you for your great work. I apologize for raising another issue, but this one has been troubling me.

When using your code to calculate Overall Consistency, specifically the cosine similarity between ViCLIP visual features and text features (line 49: vbench/overall_consistency.py), we are encountering negative results.

As we know, cosine similarity can indeed be less than 0. How do you ensure that this lower bound is exactly zero (line 55 scripts/constant.py)? Could you provide some insights on this?

Looking forward to your response.
issue_comments: @ziqihuangg
Just following up on my question. I'd appreciate any guidance when you have time. @ziqihuangg
@ziqihuangg

The lower bounds we refer to are not "theoretical", but "empirical" bounds used for normalizing the final score calculation. We have not claimed that the theoretical lower bound should be zero; in fact, it should be -1. When the ViCLIP score is below zero, it indicates that the video content and text description have "opposite semantic meaning." Positive scores suggest relevant semantics, while a score of zero typically indicates irrelevant but non-contradictory content. In the context of text-to-video generation, it is highly unusual for a video to exhibit "opposite semantic meaning" to its text prompt. Even in rare cases where this occurs, the average score across all samples would most likely still be above zero.

@scottsuk0306 yes please! I've been curious for a bit and we're building new datasets now

FYI -- my issue was the tokenization timing was specific and would require a bunch of handling or a refactor. Lmk if you figure out something clever.

Hi sorry realized I never followed up on this. @scottsuk0306 @natolambert we support HF inference in the CLoud repo which I can also add ([hf-inference](https://github.com/zankner/CLoud/blob/fac417e2c0f45fab9083bf9b77066de490e8288c/cloud/model.py#L105)). Either way, curious where we end up.
Closing as stale.

issue_title: A common interface for APIs and Models.
issue_body:
### Summary of the issue

First of all, thanks for the awesome effort for making code-evaluation-package. Highly appreciate it. However, right now, what I see is that it is integrated with just Huggingface models. It would be awesome, if we can evaluate the same for closed source models. For example something like this would be awesome:

```bash
accelerate launch  main.py \
  --model gpt-3.5-turbo \
  --max_length_generation 512 \
  --tasks instruct-humaneval \
  --instruction_tokens <user_token>,<end_token>,<assistant_token>\
  --temperature 0.2 \
  --n_samples 200 \
  --batch_size 10 \
  --allow_code_execution
``` 

So, with the same interface and the post processing logic of code-evaluation-harness, we can leverage this, to evaluate and compare code-evaluation for open source and closed source models. 

### What is the motivation

The motivation behind this is that Open-Source models are all good. However, researchers and passionate people on LLM always strive for making models that can surpass gpt in lesser number of parameters and better in performance for certain tasks. And a library like this would be really helpful.

### How can I contribute:

Well, I already have most part of this code ready. If you are aligned with the motivations of the issue, then I can create the PR. However, the problem, that I am facing is that the evaluation score for API based models are very low. For example `gpt-3.5` is giving a score for `0.006` in HumanEval benchmark. However, the generation is correct. The problem is in indentation and the post-processing of the generations. For example, one instance of the generation of `gpt-3.5.turbo` looks something like this:

```
from typing import List


def separate_paren_groups(paren_string: str) -> List[str]:
    """ Input to this function is a string containing multiple groups of nested parentheses. Your goal is to
    separate those group into separate strings and return the list of those.
    Separate groups are balanced (each open brace is properly closed) and not nested within each other
    Ignore any spaces in the input string.
    >>> separate_paren_groups('( ) (( )) (( )( ))')
    ['()', '(())', '(()())']
    """    paren_string = paren_string.replace(' ', '')
    stack = []
    result = []
    group = ''
    for char in paren_string:
        if char == '(':
            if stack:
                group += char
            stack.append(char)
        elif char == ')':
            stack.pop()
            group += char
            if not stack:
                result.append(group)
                group = ''
    return result
```
If we see the above code, then we can see, the problem is in indentation, for which during the time of evaluation, we are getting it marked as wrong. Although I tried to implement the code of big-code's post processing for different tasks. But, it was not working. So I would highly appreciate in some help there.
issue_comments:
Hi, thanks for the suggestion, I think some challenges in the evaluation of these models are that they might change & evolve behind the API which makes the evaluation numbers not very relevant over time. They also might require different post-processing to extract the code snippet since they tend to generate natural text before and after so i'm not sure the current approach we have will work out of the box for most tasks.

However if you do tests and find your implementation to work/match public numbers for certain tasks like `instruct-humaneval` or others tasks like HumanEvalsynthesize then feel free to open a PR and we can consider adding this setup for a restricted set of benchmarks if it integrates well with the codebase.

Regarding your indentation issue, I think the prompt is stripped by default and doesn't have a `\n` at the end in `instruct-humaneval`. For humaneval task we have both `humaneval` and `humaneval-unstripped` tasks because we've noticed that GPT4's tokenizer and few others like Phind require keeping the last `\n` in the prompt to work properly. Can you try evaluation again while adding the `\n`? You can do that [here](https://github.com/bigcode-project/bigcode-evaluation-harness/blob/9d17482e38eaeddee5e27488229a893dc59d42e1/bigcode_eval/utils.py#L171) or in the `context`  of the [task](https://github.com/bigcode-project/bigcode-evaluation-harness/blob/9d17482e38eaeddee5e27488229a893dc59d42e1/bigcode_eval/tasks/instruct_humaneval.py#L80)

I tried some of the things above mentioned, but everything just solved by giving a simple prompt. Does that make it a valid solution? For example for HumanEval, the problem solved when I added this prompt 

```
# make a instruction
instruction_prompt = """
Complete the given code. First write whatever is given to you, followed by just completing the rest.
Ensure you have wrote the full function. Do not Write anything else other than completing the function.\n
"""
```

And the model, I considered was `gpt-3.5-turbo` for HumanEval.

Maybe check this code that OctoCoder authors submitted for evaluation of OpenAI models on HumanEvalSynthesize  https://github.com/bigcode-project/bigcode-evaluation-harness/blob/main/bigcode_eval/tasks/humanevalpack_openai.py

> However if you do tests and find your implementation to work/match public numbers

Very interesting and weird thing, I used `gpt-3.5-turbo`, deterministic generation, pass@1 for HumanEval on that above prompt and it got me a score of 0.62, meanwhile codellama and Mistral paper states it was `48.1`.

But just adding `prompt + \n` got me a result of `0.0016`. 

One reason for this could be the time codellama evaluated and the time I am doing the evaluation, gpt-3.5 got evolved over. And I am not sure whether they are contaminated with same examples or not.
@Anindyadeep Can you open source your projec.

> @Anindyadeep Can you open source your projec.

Yeah, we will do that shortly :)

> However if you do tests and find your implementation to work/match public numbers for certain tasks like instruct-humaneval or others tasks like HumanEvalsynthesize then feel free to open a PR and we can consider adding this setup for a restricted set of benchmarks if it integrates well with the codebase.

@loubnabnl I did not checked out `instruct-humaneval` but I did checked for humaneval, and the results were greater than the results from codellama implementation and very similar with the latest 
[deepseek coder](https://github.com/deepseek-ai/deepseek-coder/).


So, can you share is the below mentioned interface is okay, if I put the PR? Feel free to suggest me changes if any.

```
python3  main.py \
  --model gpt-3.5-turbo \
  --max_length_generation 512 \
  --tasks instruct-humaneval \
  --instruction_tokens <user_token>,<end_token>,<assistant_token>\
  --temperature 0.2 \
  --n_samples 200 \
  --batch_size 10 \
  --allow_code_execution
```
Yes feel free to open a PR and add the scores you got

Hi @loubnabnl, I started a PR. Let me know which benchmarks, I need to evaluate through this so that I can add the results too. 

Thanks

issue_title: Results are not in the same order as input test cases
issue_body:
Evaluation results are out of order, which makes it a pain to use them in a downstream process, or if I'm evaluating 2 models predictions at once. The 2 model's predictions and scores get mixed up.

**Steps to reproduce the behavior**:

```
from deepeval import evaluate
from deepeval.metrics import FaithfulnessMetric
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCase

cases = []
for idx in range(3):
    cases.append(LLMTestCase(
        input=f"input{idx}",
        actual_output=f'The context says speaker {idx} is a bad person',
        retrieval_context=[f"context{idx}"])
    )

metric = FaithfulnessMetric(model='gpt-4o',include_reason=True)

results = evaluate(cases, [metric], print_results=False)

for idx,i in enumerate(results):
    print(i.input)
    print(i.actual_output)
    print(i.retrieval_context)
    print(cases[idx].retrieval_context)
    print(i.metrics_data[0].verbose_logs)
    print('\n\n')
```

**Expected behavior**
The results list should be ordered the same way as cases list.
issue_comments: I encountered the same issue. I am trying to match the metadata of the test cases in the evaluation dataset with the test results. Without the preservation of order, this is impossible..

Take a look at the `name` attribute of the `TestResult` class instance. It follows the format `test_case_i`, where `i` represents the position of the test case in the order it was passed to `evaluate()`. By extracting this number, you can rearrange the samples to match their original order.

I think the best would be, when loading the dataset with methods such as `add_test_cases_from_json_file`, to have a parameter `id_key` to be used as the test case name. 
Today the workaround is if you have multiple cases in a file, to parse it on your own and set the `LLMTestCase`. But this is a pity it should be a functionality when loading the datasets.

issue_title: Gemini model classes should not hardcode valid models
issue_body:
Currently, the `gemini_model.py` in `llms` and `mlllms` folders hardcode a list of valid model names and use that list for error checking. This is good for error checking but it's very brittle as new models come up almost every month and old models get deprecated. The list is already out of date as of today.

My suggestion is to not hard code this list at all. If a user enters the wrong or out-of-date model, the request will fail anyway. It might be harder to debug for the user why the request failed but keeping this list up to date and relying on a new release of DeepEval for every model update is not going to work either.

issue_title: Add n1n.ai API as a model provider
issue_body:
Hi DeepEval team,

We're n1n API, and we offer a robust API that provides access to 400+ large language models (LLMs), as well as multimodal generation capabilities (text, images, video, audio, etc.). Our platform makes it easy to integrate these advanced features into your project, empowering developers with powerful AI tools.

With n1n API, you can quickly leverage a comprehensive suite of AI models for diverse applications—everything from text generation to media synthesis—through simple, easy-to-use endpoints.

Adding n1n API would benefit DeepEval users by:

- Providing access to 400+ models through a single API key configuration
- Supporting all major models (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Llama, and many more)
- Offering competitive pricing with unified billing (some are lowest to 1/10 of the official price)
- Enabling easy model switching for different assistants without managing multiple API keys

### Describe the solution you'd like

Integrate n1n API as an additional AI model provider in DeepEval. n1n API is OpenAI-compatible (base URL: https://n1n.ai/v1/) and offers extended multimodal generation capabilities beyond standard text completion, including image, video, and audio generation endpoints.

### Resources

Website: https://n1n.ai/
API Documentation: https://docs.n1n.ai/
Dashboard: https://n1n.ai/console
Support: [help@n1n.ai](mailto:help@n1n.ai)

We are excited to contribute to the DeepEval ecosystem and provide users with access to the latest AI models through our robust API platform. We look forward to collaborating with the DeepEval maintainers and community to make this integration successful.
issue_comments: @n1n-api please come back when n1n gets bigger, thank you

issue_title: Move all the existing scores into Scoring
issue_body: The initial implementation of DeepEval have metrics like FactualScore, or RAGAS score etc. However, the scoring logics are wrapped under `BaseMetric` class. However, from this issue #238, the scoring function should be granular enough so that they can be used everywhere else in the code and even outside the deepeval library code.

issue_title: Question: Using DeepEval with HuggingFace LLM
issue_body:
Hello,

Do you have a working example of using DeepEval with HuggingFace LLM without having to download the model to the local environment (like using HuggingFaceEndpoint)?

Thanks.
issue_comments: @nikhilkomakula No we don't, but it should just be an API request. The important thing is the `generate` method should only accept the prompt (string) and output the generation (string)
@nikhilkomakula If you come on our discord and share some code for your HugginFaceEndpoint i can give you an example: https://discord.com/invite/a3K9c8GRGt

issue_title: Hallucination test is really a test for faithful summarization, not of hallucination-absence
issue_body: **Describe the bug** My definition of a hallucination is a fact that occurs in the output which is not in the context. As the docs say, a "contradicted context". But DeepEval's engine is motivating the LLM to consider MISSING facts as contradictions. So if the context list 10 facts about Paris, and the output lists only 5, that's a contradiction. This part of the prompt is being ignored by GPT-4: > You should FORGIVE cases where the actual output is lacking in detail, you should ONLY provide a 'no' answer if IT IS A CONTRADICTION. **To Reproduce** ```python from deepeval import assert_test import pytest from deepeval.metrics import HallucinationMetric from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCase context = [ "Paris is the capital of France.", "The city has a population of over 2.1 million people.", "The Eiffel Tower is 324 meters (1,063 feet) tall.", "Paris is home to over 150 museums.", "The Louvre Museum attracts over 10 million visitors annually.", "The River Seine runs for 13 kilometers (8 miles) through the city.", "Paris has 20 arrondissements (districts).", "The city is home to the famous Notre-Dame Cathedral.", "The Champs-Élysées is one of the most famous avenues in the world.", "Paris is known as the City of Love and the City of Light.", ] llm_response = "\n".join(context) hallucinatory_response = f"{llm_response}\nParis is the bowling capital of the world" def test_hallucination_fails(): "This test should fail because there is a hallucination added. 10 correct facts and 1 hallucination." test_case = LLMTestCase( input="", actual_output=hallucinatory_response, context=context, ) with pytest.raises(AssertionError): assert_test(test_case, [HallucinationMetric(threshold=0.1)]) def test_subsetting_succeeds(): "This test should succeed because there is no hallucination. 2 of the 10 facts are included." good_response = "\n".join(context[0:2]) test_case = LLMTestCase( input="", actual_output=good_response, context=context ) assert_test(test_case, [HallucinationMetric(threshold=0.1)]) ``` **Expected behavior** The test named `test_hallucination_fails` should get a high hallucination score because there is a blatant hallucination in it. But it passes, because so many of the facts are correct. The test named `test_subsetting_succeeds` should get a low hallucination score because every fact is in the context, but it gets a very high score. ```text ┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ ┃ Test case ┃ Metric ┃ Score ┃ Status ┃ Overall Success Rate ┃ ┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩ │ test_hallucination_fails │ │ │ │ 100.0% │ │ │ Hallucination │ 0.09 (threshold=0.1, evaluation model=gpt-4-turbo, reason=The hallucination score is │ PASSED │ │ │ │ │ 0.09 because the actual output largely aligns with the provided factual context │ │ │ │ │ │ regarding multiple widely recognized aspects of Paris, such as its landmarks, │ │ │ │ │ │ geography, and cultural attributes, with only one noted contradiction about Paris │ │ │ │ │ │ being the 'bowling capital of the world,' which is not supported by the given │ │ │ │ │ │ context. This minor inaccuracy contributes to a low but not zero hallucination │ │ │ │ │ │ score., error=None) │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ test_subsetting_succeeds │ │ │ │ 0.0% │ │ │ Hallucination │ 0.8 (threshold=0.1, evaluation model=gpt-4-turbo, reason=The score is 0.80 because │ FAILED │ │ │ │ │ the actual output includes significant factual omissions regarding key details about │ │ │ │ │ │ Paris, such as landmarks and cultural statistics, which indicates a high level of │ │ │ │ │ │ factual incompleteness relative to the context provided. Despite the output being │ │ │ │ │ │ factually correct on some basic aspects like the capital status and population, the │ │ │ │ │ │ numerous omissions contribute to a high hallucination score., error=None) │ │ │ └──────────────────────────┴───────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────┴──────────────────────┘ ``` **Screenshots** **Context (please complete the following information):** Default model: GPT-4 deepeval 0.21.36

issue_title: Error while using the most popular opensource chat models
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
I encounter 'ValueError: Evaluation LLM outputted an invalid JSON. Please use a better evaluation model.' while using most popular open source chat models in DeepEval framework. 

**To Reproduce**
```python
"""
This script attempts to use popular open source LLMs in DeepEval framework. 

1. Custom LLM class - navigate to section 'Mistral 7b example' 
    in https://docs.confident-ai.com/docs/metrics-introduction.
    NOTE - performed minor modifications to make it work for any open-source LLM. 
    NOTE - Opensource LLMs tried are - 
        "Qwen/Qwen2-72B-Instruct"  (#1 on HF leaderboard for chat models)
        "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct" (#2 on HF leaderboard for chat models)
        "mistralai/Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct-v0.1" 
        "mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1" (recommended by DeepEval docs)
        "microsoft/Phi-3-medium-4k-instruct" (#5 on HF leaderboard for chat models)

2. Unit test is taken from -
    https://github.com/confident-ai/deepeval?tab=readme-ov-file#writing-your-first-test-case
    NOTE - set model param in test_case function to the open-source LLM of user's choice. 
"""

from deepeval import assert_test
from deepeval.metrics import AnswerRelevancyMetric
from deepeval.models.base_model import DeepEvalBaseLLM
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCase
from huggingface_hub import login
import os 
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

# wrapper class around HuggingFace API
class CustomLLM(DeepEvalBaseLLM):
    def __init__(self, name, model, tokenizer):
        self.name = name
        self.model = model
        self.tokenizer = tokenizer

    def load_model(self):
        return self.model

    def generate(self, prompt: str) -> str:
        model = self.load_model()
        device = "cuda" # the device to load the model onto
        model_inputs = self.tokenizer([prompt], return_tensors="pt").to(device)
        generated_ids = model.generate(**model_inputs, max_new_tokens=100)
        return self.tokenizer.batch_decode(generated_ids)[0]

    async def a_generate(self, prompt: str) -> str:
        return self.generate(prompt)

    def get_model_name(self):
        return self.name 

# step 0 - user input
cuda_available_devices = "<add your visible devices>"
cache_dir="<add your cache dir>"
hf_token = "<add your HF token>"

# step 1 - set environment variables
os.environ['CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES'] = cuda_available_devices
os.environ['HF_HOME'] = cache_dir
login(token=hf_token, add_to_git_credential=True)

# step 2 -  run unit test case with open source LLM of your choice
def wrap_hf_api(name, cache_dir):
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(name, 
                                                 device_map="auto", 
                                                cache_dir=cache_dir)
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(name, cache_dir=cache_dir)
    llm = CustomLLM(name=name, model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer)
    return llm

def test_case():
    test_case = LLMTestCase(
        input="What if these shoes don't fit?",
        actual_output="We offer a 30-day full refund at no extra costs.",
        retrieval_context=["All customers are eligible for a 30 day full refund at no extra costs."]
    )
    llm = wrap_hf_api("mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1", cache_dir)
    answer_relevancy_metric = AnswerRelevancyMetric(threshold=0.5, model=llm)
    assert_test(test_case, [answer_relevancy_metric])
```

**Expected behavior**
After running `deepeval test run test_chatbot.py`, I get `ValueError: Evaluation LLM outputted an invalid JSON. Please use a better evaluation model.` for all 5 opensource models I tested. 

**Desktop (please complete the following information):**
 - OS: Ubuntu 20.10

**Additional context**
DeepEval version is 0.21.64.
issue_comments:
This issue is caused by expecting JSON output from the LLM. The prompt template provides instructions for the LLM to provide JSON output. However, you cannot expect the model to return JSON every time. Even more complex models like GPT4 can sometimes make mistakes. 
Contributors could try to solve it by integrating, for example, Instructor https://github.com/jxnl/instructor/ in DeepEval
@penguine-ip  Is it possible to take a look at this issue please ?

Hi @penguine-ip , it is not guaranteed that all LLMs return us in expected format. It might stop at producing complete output due to default max tolken limit. There might be change in code in  https://github.com/confident-ai/deepeval/blob/main/deepeval/models/llms/utils.py

issue_title: Issues with Llama 3
issue_body:
If you are having issues with Llama3 or any of its versions, here's the most straightforward to get it running:

1) Use this as your model file for Llama3 (make sure to use the name for the version you've pulled):

```
FROM llama3:8b
TEMPLATE """{{ if .System }}<|start_header_id|>system<|end_header_id|>

{{ .System }}<|eot_id|>{{ end }}{{ if .Prompt }}<|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>

{{ .Prompt }}<|eot_id|>{{ end }}<|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>

{{ .Response }}<|eot_id|>"""
PARAMETER stop "<|start_header_id|>"
PARAMETER stop "<|end_header_id|>"
PARAMETER stop "<|eot_id|>"
PARAMETER stop "<|reserved_special_token|>"
PARAMETER stop "assistant"
PARAMETER stop ".assistant"
PARAMETER stop "!assistant"
PARAMETER stop "?assistant"
```
2) On the `settings` model in Ollama Grid Search, make sure to **completely** remove the `stop` parameter from the "default options" textarea:

![image](https://github.com/dezoito/ollama-grid-search/assets/6494010/5db22760-563a-465d-bdbb-45204c100c28)
(The above is what I am currently using)


**But why is this necessary?**

As far as I can understand, Llama3 requires these specific stop parameters, but there were some issues or misspellings with the models provided from Ollama's library (maybe an upstream issue).

If those are not setup correctly (and you don't specify a `num_predict`  option) , the model will not stop generating tokens (and thus run indefinitely or timeout).

If you pass the API anything for the `stop` parameter, it will try to use that, instead of what's in the `modelfile`, and run into the same previous issues.

That was not a requirement for **any** of the previous models I've tested (and that's a long list), but currently this is the best way to deal with it.


@zedmango

issue_title: Two issues while installing current master branch and their workarounds
issue_body:
Thank you for the excellent package. The current master branch is having some issues installing. Here are the issues and their workarounds. I already saw a PR for `pynndescent`. I'm not sure if `milvus` workaround is ok. I'll report back if I encounter any other issue. Admin, pls close this issue once the master has been updated to handle these two. Thanks.

**Issue 1:**
`pynndescent` dockerfile requirements are outdated.

**Workaround:**
Update `install/Dockerfile.pynndescent` as below

```
FROM ann-benchmarks

RUN pip3 install 'numba==0.46' 'llvmlite==0.33.0' 'numpy==1.17' scikit-learn
RUN pip3 install pynndescent
RUN python3 -c 'import pynndescent'
```

**Issue 2:**
milvus `server_config.yaml` link is broken

**Workaround:**
Update install/Dockerfile.milvus as below

```
# Install Milvus
FROM milvusdb/milvus:0.6.0-cpu-d120719-2b40dd as milvus
RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install -y wget
RUN wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/milvus-io/docs/master/v0.6.0/assets/server_config.yaml
RUN sed -i 's/cpu_cache_capacity: 16/cpu_cache_capacity: 4/' server_config.yaml  # otherwise my Docker blows up
RUN mv server_config.yaml /var/lib/milvus/conf/server_config.yaml

# Switch back to ANN-benchmarks base image and copy all files
FROM ann-benchmarks
COPY --from=milvus /var/lib/milvus /var/lib/milvus
ENV LD_LIBRARY_PATH="${LD_LIBRARY_PATH}:/var/lib/milvus/lib"
RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install -y libmysqlclient-dev

# Python client
RUN pip3 install pymilvus==0.2.7

# Fixing some version incompatibility thing
RUN pip3 install numpy==1.18 scipy==1.1.0 scikit-learn==0.21

# Dumb entrypoint thing that runs the daemon as well
RUN echo '#!/bin/bash' >> entrypoint.sh
RUN echo '/var/lib/milvus/bin/milvus_server -d -c /var/lib/milvus/conf/server_config.yaml -l /var/lib/milvus/conf/log_config.conf' >> entrypoint.sh
RUN echo 'sleep 5' >> entrypoint.sh
RUN echo 'python3 run_algorithm.py "$@"' >> entrypoint.sh
RUN chmod u+x entrypoint.sh
ENTRYPOINT ["/home/app/entrypoint.sh"]
```

issue_comments: I can't make much out of these logs. Without more info, I guess I can only say that you should try running them individually (i.e., not all algos at once). If they pass then it's probably some sort of resource constraint that occurs when you run multiple at once. If they fail, then at least the logs will hopefully be more understandable.

A bunch of algos will fail on every dataset unfortunately – I'd say 80-90%+ will succeed though.

Like @maumueller said, in the case of Glove it might be because the dimensionality is not divisible by 4

> Force Merge...
> ...
> urllib3.exceptions.ReadTimeoutError: HTTPConnectionPool(host='localhost', port=9200): Read timed out. (read timeout=1000)

I think it's timing out on the force merge step. So all vectors have been indexed, it's trying to, basically, "optimize" the index, but it's taking too long.

@fcakir @maumueller Hi, I am the maintainer of the Vald project.
Unfortunately, current Vald docker image does not support non-AVX2 environments.
The CPU Xeon E5-1607 v2 does not support AVX2, so it has been confirmed that Vald does not work properly.

The successor CPU, Xeon E5-1607 v3, supports AVX2 and will work.
FYI: https://technical.city/en/cpu/Xeon-E5-1607-v3-vs-Xeon-E5-1607-v2

Since we reused the official Vald docker image for this part when running the ann-benchmark, I will submit a pull request for the change at a later date, since it is necessary to rebuild from source code when using Vald in a non-AVX2 environment.

issue_comments:
``````python
import vertexai
import asyncio
import tenacity
import os
import argparse
import time
from evalplus.data import get_human_eval_plus, get_mbpp_plus, write_jsonl
from google.api_core.exceptions import InternalServerError, ResourceExhausted
from vertexai.preview.generative_models import GenerativeModel
from typing import Sequence, Any, TypeVar, Iterable

_T = TypeVar("_T")


def chunked(seq: Sequence[_T], n: int) -> Iterable[Sequence[_T]]:
    """Yield successive n-sized chunks from seq."""
    return (seq[i : i + n] for i in range(0, len(seq), n))


def retry(errors: Any, max_attempts: int = 5):
    return tenacity.retry(
        retry=tenacity.retry_if_exception_type(errors),
        wait=tenacity.wait_exponential(multiplier=1, min=5, max=20),
        stop=tenacity.stop_after_attempt(max_attempts),
        before_sleep=print,
    )


PROJECT_ID = os.getenv("GOOGLE_API_PROJECT_ID")
LOCATION = os.getenv("GOOGLE_API_LOCATION", "us-central1")


@retry((IndexError, ResourceExhausted, InternalServerError))
async def gemini_chat_completions_with_backoff(
    model_name: str,
    messages: list[dict[str, str]],
):
    assert model_name == "gemini-pro"
    model = GenerativeModel(model_name)
    contents = [
        {"role": message["role"], "parts": [{"text": message["content"]}]}
        for message in messages
    ]
    response = await model.generate_content_async(
        contents,
        generation_config={
            "temperature": 0.0,
            "top_p": 1.0,
        },
        safety_settings={0: 0, 1: 0, 2: 0, 3: 0, 4: 0},
    )
    candidate = response.candidates[0]
    return candidate.text


PROMPT_TEMPLATE = """Please generate code to complete the following problem. 
```python
{prompt}
```
Enclose your solution in triple backticks."""


async def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--path", type=str)
    parser.add_argument("--dataset", type=str, choices=["humaneval", "mbpp"])
    parser.add_argument(
        "--rpm", type=int, default=45, help="Requests per minute to send to the API"
    )

    vertexai.init(project=PROJECT_ID, location=LOCATION)

    args = parser.parse_args()
    problems = get_human_eval_plus() if args.dataset == "humaneval" else get_mbpp_plus()

    batches = chunked(list(problems.values()), n=args.rpm)
    for index, batch in enumerate(batches):
        if index != 0:
            print("Sleeping for 60 seconds")
            time.sleep(60)
        print(f"Batch {index}")
        tasks = list[Any]()
        for problem in batch:
            raw_prompt = problem["prompt"].strip()
            prompt = PROMPT_TEMPLATE.format(prompt=raw_prompt)
            messages = [
                {"role": "user", "content": prompt},
            ]
            tasks.append(gemini_chat_completions_with_backoff("gemini-pro", messages))
        task_results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
        results = list[dict]()
        for problem, result in zip(batch, task_results):
            if isinstance(result, Exception):
                print("Error in processing", problem["task_id"])
                completion = ":clown:"
            else:
                completion = result
            # extract the code
            raw_prompt = problem["prompt"].strip()
            try:
                completion = completion.split("```")[1]
                if completion.startswith("python"):
                    completion = completion[6:]
                solution = f"{raw_prompt}\n{completion}"
                results.append({"task_id": problem["task_id"], "solution": solution})
            except IndexError:
                results.append(
                    {"task_id": problem["task_id"], "completion": completion}
                )
        write_jsonl(args.path, results, append=True)


asyncio.run(main())
``````

Unofficial results with the code above:
```
humaneval (base tests)
pass@1: 0.634
humaneval+ (base + extra tests)
pass@1: 0.555
```
@UniverseFly Thanks! Would you also help evaluate the MBPP scores and I can update them soon.

@ganler Code updated above.

import logging

def create_evidently_project(workspace_name: str, project_name: str) -> Project:

issue_title: New pydantic version crashes import resolving
issue_body:
### Description

Yesterday, a compatibility fix was released in Pydantic version 1, which added the "v1" submodule in versions 1.., causing the try/except feature to stop working. 

Pydantic release: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/9157

Evidently code https://github.com/evidentlyai/evidently/blob/v0.4.18/src/evidently/_pydantic_compat.py#L7

```python
try:
    from pydantic import v1  # noqa

    v = 2
except ImportError:
    v = 1
```

> This bug can be reproduced ONLY if you have other packages installed before evidently with an unfixed Pydantic version, such as "<2" or "1.10", etc.

### Steps to reproduce:
1. Install pydantic==1.10.15.
2. Install evidently.
3. Try to build a classification report, for example, or perform an import:
```python
classification_performance_report = Report(
        metrics=[
            ClassificationPreset(),
        ]
    )
classification_performance_report.run(reference_data=None, current_data=df)
html = classification_performance_report.get_html()
```

issue_title: Target drift report with real label only
issue_body: how can i use the target drift detect  for feature, i don't find in the doc.
issue_comments:
Hi @xuyunlong123, could you clarify what you try to achieve?

If you only have a single column (real label) and want to see the shift in its distribution, you can simply create a `Report` with a single `ColumnDriftMetric` for this column.

If you want to detect drift for a set of features, you use `DataDriftPreset`.

The `TargetDriftPreset` is useful when you have both prediction/label column and feature columns and you want to look at data drift as well as on correlation changes / visually explore the dependency between target and features.

> Hi @xuyunlong123, could you clarify what you try to achieve?
> 
> If you only have a single column (real label) and want to see the shift in its distribution, you can simply create a `Report` with a single `ColumnDriftMetric` for this column.
> 
> If you want to detect drift for a set of features, you use `DataDriftPreset`.
> 
> The `TargetDriftPreset` is useful when you have both prediction/label column and feature columns and you want to look at data drift as well as on correlation changes / visually explore the dependency between target and features.

emm，i see. the situation is that i creat many creaditscore models for loan application,but they have the same target label and different features, so i want to use one detector which can test the drift between features and target.That confuses me.Thanks for your reply.

> Hi @xuyunlong123, could you clarify what you try to achieve?
> 
> If you only have a single column (real label) and want to see the shift in its distribution, you can simply create a `Report` with a single `ColumnDriftMetric` for this column.
> 
> If you want to detect drift for a set of features, you use `DataDriftPreset`.
> 
> The `TargetDriftPreset` is useful when you have both prediction/label column and feature columns and you want to look at data drift as well as on correlation changes / visually explore the dependency between target and features.

I have another question if i only have label and  feature columns, the `TargetDriftPreset` could work?
Hi @xuyunlong123, yes - you just need to label your `label` column as target in column mapping https://docs.evidentlyai.com/user-guide/input-data/column-mapping#target-and-prediction

issue_title: `DriftDriftPreset()` takes 8 hours for data with 3M rows and 1k columns
issue_body:
Originally my reference data have 10M rows and current data have 1M rows, and I am computing data drift using evidently - but the process gets killed (possibly due to OOM).
I then downsample my reference data to 30% - so reference data have 3M rows and current data have 0.3M rows - but the process takes 8 hours to run.
Note: There are 1k columns.
My code:
```python
from evidently.metric_preset import DataDriftPreset
from evidently.report import Report
 
report = Report(metrics=[DataDriftPreset()])
report.run(reference_data=reference_data, current_data=current_data)

drift_report = report.as_dict()
is_data_drift_present = drift_report["metrics"][0]["result"]["dataset_drift"]

drifted_features = [
            feature_name
            for feature_name, drift_info in drift_report["metrics"][1]["result"][
                "drift_by_columns"
            ].items()
            if drift_info["drift_detected"]
        ]
```


Is there a way to fasten data drift computation to reduce runtime from 8hrs to 2hrs? 

Would it be worthwhile to look at parallel execution at column level? Or can I just specify additional in evidently function call to fasten it?
issue_comments:
Hi @iamharshit,

For data drift detection, you can take even a smaller sample. This is often effective because you typically don’t need to analyze all records to identify distribution shifts.

Another option is to generate per-column checks separately using column drift detection. You can then extract individual drift scores or test results for each column in Python. This allows you to split the process into multiple smaller steps instead of running everything at once.

It looks like you’re using the older Evidently API, where the metric is called `ColumnDriftMetric()`.
In the newer version, the equivalent is `ValueDrift()`:
👉 https://docs.evidentlyai.com/metrics/all_metrics#data-drift

```
from dataclasses import asdict
import uuid
from evidently.model.dashboard import DashboardInfo
dashboard_id = "evidently_dashboard_" + str(uuid.uuid4()).replace("-", "")
tab_widgets = [t.info() for t in data_drift_report.tabsData]
di = DashboardInfo(dashboard_id, [item for tab in tab_widgets for item in tab if item is not None])
```
and calling any of the objects in tab_widgets/di/asdict(di) crashes the jupyter notebook instance
issue_comments:
ah fixed the issue just now. In windows the pandas dataframe columns have value types float64 and float32 as opposed to just Mac with float only. When you have nan values the colume type is defaulted to float32 so after you fillna in the dataframe you will need to explicitly change the colume types for json to work!

feel free to close this issue as this is more of a windows issue that one needs to deal with all data science projects when you want to dump something into json
one less ideal consequence of having a big dataset is that the html file I generated is 1G large

Hi,

Glad it was resolved quickly! 

Thanks for opening the issue. Even though it is solvable with a work-around, we will add to the list of bugs to address. 

Let us know if you need any further help or have any feedback on the tool.
this article might be helpful for the fix https://ellisvalentiner.com/post/serializing-numpyfloat32-json/

issue_title: Chi-square fails on categorical data
issue_body:
I tried using the chi-squared test, but I observed that it only works on numerical columns. When I try using categoricals, it fails. Here's how to reproduce. 

```python
def synthetic_interaction_data(n, yintercept = 10):
    df = pd.DataFrame()
    df["id"] = range(n)
    df[f'x1'] = np.random.random(size=n)*10
    df[f'x2'] = np.random.random(size=n)*10
    df[f'x3'] = np.random.random(size=n)*10
    df['target'] = (df['x1']**2 + df['x1']*df['x2'] + 5*df['x1']*np.sin(3*df['x2'])  + yintercept).astype("int16") % 2
    return df

df = synthetic_interaction_data(2000)
df["odd"] = "even"
df.loc[np.floor(df["x1"]) % 2 == 1, "odd"] = "odd"
df['target'] = df['target'].astype(np.object)

df2 = synthetic_interaction_data(2000)
df2["odd"] = "even"
df2.loc[np.floor(df2["x1"]) % 2 == 1, "odd"] = "odd"
df2['target'] = df2['target'].astype(np.object)

ddp = Profile(sections=[DataDriftProfileSection])
ddp.calculate(df, df2, column_mapping = None)
```

Shows the error below:
```

Hi @mabreyes thanks for noting, and glad you solved the issue! For now, that is exactly how this feature works: one needs to encode categorical features with numerical labels. We expect to adapt the tool to work with categorical data at a later point. Here is a step by step guide that covers some of the pre-processing steps one might need to perform for some reports/data types: https://docs.evidentlyai.com/step-by-step-guide-for-jupyter-notebooks
@elenasamuylova Thanks so much. Closing this issue for now.

issue_title: Some of the Test are not working
issue_body: Some of the Tests are not working. The names are not correct ? or they are not present in the module anymore ? If the have been changed (renamed) can you provide a list of the tests with the old and new name ? Thanks.
issue_comments:
Hi @samuelamico, could you check which Evidenly version you are using?

`TestNumberOfNulls` (and similar null-related tests) were renamed to `TestNumberOfMissingValues` in one of the earlier releases.

You can see the **log of changes** in the release notes, but it might be that something was missed: 
https://github.com/evidentlyai/evidently/releases

Sorry for the confusion, if any!

The current Evidently version (0.2.0 onwards) is considered stable; there should be no more changes to the names of existing tests. To **see the current names** of all tests, you can check the "All tests" example notebooks (Jupyter: https://github.com/evidentlyai/evidently/blob/main/examples/sample_notebooks/evidently_tests.ipynb), consult the API reference or "All tests" table: https://docs.evidentlyai.com/reference/all-tests
Thanks for the update.

issue_title: TestColumnsType -> extract exact column that made the test fail
issue_body:
Hi,

Started to try your package and this amazing !
Have two questions

Q1:
Tried to use the TestSuite with number of tests.
For example the 'TestColumnsType' was failed ( changed it manually ) , how can I extract the **exact column**  that made the test fail?

My tests :

set_of_tests_for_data_quality =\
TestSuite(tests=[
     evi_tests.TestColumnsType(), 
  
])

Q2:
Using the same data ( with mismatched column type ) the report was crushed . 

Error => ValueError: Column Total_intl_calls have different types in reference int64 and current float16

I assumed it would show me the problematic column.

data_drift_report = Report(metrics=[ DataDriftPreset(),]) 
data_drift_report.run(reference_data = train_df , current_data= test_df, column_mapping=column_mapping) 

Will appreciate your response,
Boris
issue_comments:
Hi @borisRa, 

**1. Extracting the names of columns with type mismatch.** 

If you generate the visual test suite output (HTML file or directly, e.g., in the Jupyter notebook) for this test, on expand, it contains a table that shows each expected/actual column type. You can explore it visually to see for which columns it failed. 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/67064421/225712183-54f89461-6a98-4943-8efc-e74ec6eb2b78.png)

If you want to extract the list programmatically, you’d need to parse the JSON / Python dictionary output. 

(Here is how to get the output of the test suite as Python dictionary or JSON https://docs.evidentlyai.com/user-guide/tests-and-reports/run-tests#output-formats)

The easiest way is to get the output as a Python dictionary and extract the necessary information using Python.

**2. Data drift report** 

Data drift report performs statistical tests / calculates distance metrics to detect the difference in feature distributions between two datasets. It expects the data schema and column types to match; otherwise, it is not possible to perform the computation. 

It is expected behavior; this report/metric is not meant to help discover data quality issues (with that purpose, it is best to use tests from the data quality/data integrity group).

Thanks @elenasamuylova !

About **Extracting the names of columns with type mismatch** :  Is there a way to get mismatched columns directly without looping through all columns ?

About  **Data drift report** : I tried to use the `TestColumnsType` test but got this error : 
`ValueError: Column Total_intl_calls have different types in reference int64 and current float16` I expected to get this test fail but not crash.

My code is :
```

set_of_tests = TestSuite(tests=[ evi_tests.TestColumnsType(),])		   
set_of_tests.run(reference_data=train_df, current_data=test_df)
```

Hi @borisRa, 

1. Right now, you can only work with the existing JSON/Python dict output, so all further transformations must happen externally. (There will be some changes to the JSON format of all metrics/tests in the near future to make it easier to parse). 

2. Sorry, I must have misunderstood your question since the code snippet mentioned `DataDriftPreset()`. Don't have an immediate answer here as to why this error might come up with `TestColumnsType` test. 

Does it occur when you run a single `TestColumnsType`  test in a test suite, or do you have multiple tests in a suite? 

CC @Liraim and @emeli-dral to take a look when they can!

Thanks @elenasamuylova  ,

About "Does it occur when you run a single TestColumnsType test in a test suite, or do you have multiple tests in a suite?" 
Both as a single test showed above and as multiple test.

Hi @borisRa,

Re: TestColumnsType test throwing an error: we ran a number of tests (e.g., changing the int64 to float16 in current data) but could not reproduce the error. Is there a chance you could share a few lines from your dataframe, including nulls if any?
@borisRa Quick question: what evidently version do you use? I believe we cannot reproduce the issue, because it was solved in the 0.2.7 version

Hi @emeli-dral ,

Thanks this issue was resolved !
Though a new one was  arrived :)

`set_of_tests_for_data_quality.save_html(path)`

Getting this error => `AttributeError: 'TestResult' object has no attribute 'features'`

hey @borisRa ,
could you please also share with us the full stack trace error?

We struggle to reproduce this one as well.
